title: Types of Eddy Current Sensing Probes
narrationScript: At Level One you learned where each coil goes. At Level Two you choose the probe and defend the choice, because a flaw the probe cannot see never becomes data. Every probe is three decisions. Configuration — surface, encircling, or bobbin — decides where the currents flow, and a flaw signals only if it cuts them. Winding and mode decide what survives to the screen. An absolute coil responds to everything, including drift. A differential pair cancels what both coils share, drawing its two lobed signature on local flaws while hiding gradual thinning. A reflection probe separates driver from pickup, buying a wide frequency range and thermal stability. A coil senses the rate of change of flux, so its output falls as frequency drops. A Hall element reads flux density directly, flat down to DC, which is why deep penetration work at very low frequency leans on field sensors. When orientation beats you — a bobbin against circumferential cracks — change the coil, not the settings: rotating pancake and array probes exist for that job.

sections.heading: The Probe Is the First Evaluation Decision
sections.body:
At Level I you scanned with whatever the technique sheet specified. Level II is different: you help select the probe and you defend the selection — because a flaw the probe cannot put on the screen never becomes data at all. No amount of gain, filtering, or analysis recovers it downstream.

Every probe is three stacked decisions:

- **Configuration** — *where the coil sits*. A **surface (probe) coil**, axis normal to the surface, drives circular current loops directly beneath the winding. An **encircling (OD) coil** that the product feeds through, and a **bobbin (ID) coil** working from the bore, both drive **circumferential** currents. Configuration fixes where the currents flow, and the Level I master rule still governs everything: **a discontinuity signals in proportion to how strongly it cuts the induced current paths**.
- **Winding arrangement and operating mode** — *how the coil is built and connected*: impedance or reflection windings; absolute or differential connection. This decides which part of the raw response survives to the display.
- **Sensing element** — *what physically senses the field*: an induction coil, or a solid-state field sensor such as a Hall element.

Construction details ride along. A **ferrite core** concentrates flux so a small coil keeps usable sensitivity, and a **ferrite shield** — a cup around the winding — confines the field laterally, letting the probe work beside edges, fastener heads, and geometry changes without drowning the flaw signal in edge effect.

sections.caption: The three winding-and-mode building blocks: an absolute coil answers to everything including drift; a differential pair cancels shared responses and writes a two-lobed signature on local flaws; a reflection probe separates driver from pickup for wide frequency range and thermal stability.

sections.heading: Winding Arrangements and Operating Modes
sections.body:
Keep two ideas separate that Level I often blurs: how the windings are **built**, and how they are **connected**.

**Built — the winding arrangement:**

- **Impedance type.** One winding drives the field and senses the response; the instrument reads the coil's own impedance change. Simple and rugged — the classic arrangement.
- **Reflection (driver–pickup, send–receive).** Separate driver and pickup windings, commonly a larger driver with small pickups inside it. Each winding is optimized for its own job, so one probe holds sensitivity across a **wide frequency range** — the natural choice for multifrequency work — and because the output is the pickup's induced voltage rather than the driven coil's impedance, the probe is noticeably more stable against **thermal drift**.
- **Through-transmission.** Driver on one side of a thin sheet, pickup on the other. Full-thickness interrogation, but only where both surfaces are accessible and the field can penetrate the section.

**Connected — the operating mode:**

- **Absolute.** A single sensing winding, usually balanced against a reference winding that never sees the part. It responds to *everything*: local flaws, gradual wall thinning, conductivity shifts, temperature, lift-off or fill-factor change. Total information — and total exposure to drift.
- **Differential, self-comparison.** Two sensing windings side by side on the same part, connected in opposition. Whatever both coils see alike cancels. A short local flaw passes one coil and then the other, writing the characteristic **two-lobed, figure-eight** trace on the impedance plane.
- **Differential, external reference.** The second coil sits on a separate reference standard, so the pair reports any part-versus-standard difference, gradual ones included — the sorting arrangement.

The axes combine freely. A rotating bolt-hole probe is typically a **reflection-differential hybrid**: driver winding, differential pickup pair.

sections.title: Every mode has a blind spot
sections.body: Differential self-comparison cancels whatever both coils see alike — which kills temperature drift, but also hides **gradual wall thinning** and the middle of a **long, uniform flaw** (only its ends signal as they enter and leave the coil pair). Absolute sees all of that, but **drifts** with temperature and wobbles with lift-off. That is why in-service tubing examinations record **absolute and differential channels simultaneously**, and why mode is chosen from the flaw you expect — never from habit.

sections.heading: Matching the Probe: Geometry First, Then Orientation
sections.body:
Probe matching is two questions, asked in order. **Can the configuration reach the region of interest?** Installed heat-exchanger tubing offers only the bore — bobbin. Product running on a mill line feeds through a ring — encircling. Open surfaces belong to surface probes. **Then: will the induced currents be cut by the flaw you expect?** Draw the current paths, draw the flaw, check the crossing.

Tubing shows why the second question has teeth. A bobbin's currents are **circumferential**, so it is strong on pitting, wall loss, and **longitudinal** cracking — and weak on **circumferential** cracks, which lie parallel to its currents. Circumferential cracking concentrates exactly where that hurts: tube support plates, expansion transitions, U-bends. No mode, gain, or frequency change fixes an orientation problem; the fix is a different coil:

- **Rotating pancake coils (RPC/MRPC)** — small surface coils spun against the wall while the probe advances, scanning a helix. Their small circular loops are cut by cracks of **any surface orientation**, and rotation resolves **where around the circumference** the flaw sits — the answer a bobbin can never give. The price is time: minutes per tube instead of seconds, so rotating probes are typically targeted at known degradation locations.
- **Array probes** — a ring of small coils multiplexed electronically: most of that coverage at far higher speed, with no moving parts.
- **Orthogonal ('plus-point') coils** — two windings crossed at right angles and connected differentially; a linear flaw in either orientation unbalances the pair, while shared responses — including much of the lift-off wobble — cancel.

The same logic runs on the production line: an encircling coil is a **longitudinal-seam hunter**, the center of solid bar is blind at any frequency, and purely circumferential cracks call for a supplementary technique.

sections.caption: Orientation matching in tubing: a bobbin's circumferential currents run parallel to a circumferential crack (weak or missed), while a rotating pancake coil's small loops are cut by cracks of any surface orientation — and rotation reports position around the tube.

sections.heading: Aerospace Surface Work: Holes, Layers, and Edges
sections.body:
Aerospace structure asks three recurring questions, and each has a standard probe answer.

- **Fastener holes.** Bore fatigue cracks start small — often a corner crack at the faying surface — and grow radially. The standard tool is a **motorized rotating scanner** spinning a small, shielded, usually reflection-differential surface coil at hundreds to a few thousand rpm while it advances through the hole, covering the bore in a tight helix. The small coil concentrates sensitivity on millimeter-scale cracks, the shield keeps hole-entry and layer-interface edges from swamping the signal, and rotation reports the crack's clock position.
- **Buried layers.** Lap joints and doublers crack at fastener rows in the **second or third layer**, far below a high-frequency probe's reach. Penetration grows as frequency falls — in a 2024-series aluminum skin (≈30 %IACS), the standard depth of penetration is roughly **0.4 mm at 100 kHz** but about **3.8 mm at 1 kHz** — so buried-layer work runs at **hundreds of hertz to a few kilohertz**. At those frequencies an ordinary impedance coil's output is feeble, which is why the standard hardware is **reflection type**: **sliding probes** (driver–pickup pairs scanned rapidly along the fastener row) and **ring probes** (centered on each fastener in turn).
- **Edges and fasteners.** An unshielded coil's field reaches well beyond its housing, so it begins answering to an edge before the probe arrives there. **Shielded probes** confine the field so cracks at fastener heads, hole edges, skin edges, and radii stay readable.

The running trade in all three: the smaller and better-shielded the coil, the higher the sensitivity and the smaller the footprint — and the more passes complete coverage costs.

sections.caption: The configuration-and-mode matrix: six recurring jobs and the probe that matches.
rows: Surface cracks on open skin or weld toe
Shielded surface probe (pancake/pencil)
Impedance; absolute or differential
Circular loops are cut by surface cracks at any orientation; shield tames edges
Bolt hole, fastener removed
Small shielded surface coil on rotating scanner
Reflection-differential hybrid
Helical bore coverage; finds short corner cracks; reports clock position
Second-layer crack at lap-joint fastener row
Sliding or ring probe over the row
Reflection, low frequency (hundreds of Hz–few kHz)
Low frequency reaches the buried layer; pickup keeps output usable
In-service condenser tubing, full length
Bobbin (ID) coil
Absolute + differential recorded together
Bore is the only access; differential flags pits, absolute tracks thinning
Circumferential cracking at tube supports
Rotating pancake or array probe
Driver–pickup, differential/multiplexed
Small loops cross the crack; resolves position around the tube
Bar, rod, wire screened at the mill
Encircling (OD) coil
Differential self-comparison
Longitudinal seams cut the circumferential currents; line speed does the scanning

sections.heading: Coil or Hall Element?
sections.body:
Everything above sensed the field with a winding, and a winding obeys Faraday's law: output voltage proportional to the **rate of change of flux** — for sinusoidal fields, proportional to **frequency × flux density**. That single fact decides the matchup.

- **Induction coil.** At conventional test frequencies — tens of kilohertz to megahertz — a coil delivers excellent sensitivity from a passive, rugged, inexpensive element. But cut the frequency tenfold and the same coil's output drops tenfold; at the very low frequencies that deep penetration demands, coil output sinks toward the noise floor.
- **Hall element.** A thin semiconductor slab carrying a bias current develops a voltage proportional to **flux density itself: Vₕ ∝ I × B**, regardless of how fast the field changes. Its response is flat **from DC** up through typical test frequencies, the element can be made well under a millimeter across, and it packs naturally into arrays. The costs: it needs bias current and amplification, and at conventional ET frequencies its raw sensitivity generally does not match a good coil's.

The Level II rule of thumb follows: **the coil is the workhorse of conventional eddy current testing; as frequency falls toward DC — thick sections, far-surface flaws in multilayer structure, field measurement under magnetic saturation — solid-state field sensors take over.** In practice the Hall element usually serves as the **pickup in a reflection arrangement**: a wound driver supplies the field, the solid-state element reads the response. Magnetoresistive (MR/GMR) sensors occupy the same niche with the same flat-to-DC argument.

Close every probe decision the same way: one sentence each for configuration, winding and mode, and sensing element. If you cannot say all three, the probe was chosen by habit — not by the flaw.

figures.title: Bobbin vs rotating pancake on circumferential cracks

interactive.title: Probe Selection Clinic
interactive.intro: Four jobs from real tubing and airframe work. For each one, pick the probe — configuration, mode, element — that the flaw demands. Decide before you click.

steps.prompt: A condenser tube bundle has a history of slow, uniform wall thinning over long lengths. Last outage's differential bobbin data showed almost nothing. What catches it this time?
options: Add 6 dB of gain to the differential channel
Record and evaluate an absolute bobbin channel alongside the differential
Pull the bobbin probe faster to sharpen the response
Raise the test frequency
steps.feedback: Self-comparison differential cancels whatever both coils share — and gradual thinning is shared almost perfectly, so gain just amplifies a cancelled signal. The absolute channel tracks slow wall loss, which is why in-service tubing exams record both channels at once.

steps.prompt: Outside-surface stress-corrosion cracking is suspected at the tube support plates — circumferentially oriented. Your bobbin shows little. The fix is:
options: Switch the bobbin from differential to absolute
Lower the frequency for deeper penetration
Run a rotating pancake or array probe at the support-plate elevations
Use a larger bobbin to improve fill factor
steps.feedback: This is an orientation problem: the bobbin's circumferential currents lie parallel to a circumferential crack at any mode, frequency, or fill factor. Small pancake coils drive loops the crack must cut — and the rotating or array scan also tells you where around the tube it sits.

steps.prompt: Fatigue cracks are suspected at a fastener row in the second layer of a fuselage lap joint, about 3 mm down. Best tool:
options: A 2 MHz shielded pencil probe scanned along the row
A through-transmission pair across the joint
An absolute conductivity probe and meter
A low-frequency reflection probe — sliding or ring type — over the fastener row
steps.feedback: At 2 MHz in this aluminum the standard depth of penetration is under 0.1 mm — first-layer only. Through-transmission needs access to both sides, which an assembled joint denies. Low frequency (hundreds of hertz to a few kilohertz) reaches the second layer, and the reflection winding keeps the output usable down there.

steps.prompt: A wing-attach bolt hole has just had its fastener removed for inspection. The standard examination is:
options: A large unshielded pancake coil centered over the hole mouth
A small shielded differential coil on a motorized rotating scanner, run through the bore
A bobbin coil sized to the hole, pulled through once
Reinstall the fastener and scan around its head at high frequency
steps.feedback: The rotating shielded coil covers the bore in a tight helix with sensitivity to millimeter-scale corner cracks and reports their clock position. A surface coil at the hole mouth mostly reads edge effect, a single bobbin pull averages the full circumference and blunts sensitivity to small corner cracks, and scanning over an installed fastener inspects the fastener — not the bore.

checkQuestions.question: A differential self-comparison bobbin probe passes a short, isolated corrosion pit in a condenser tube. The classic signal is:
options: A permanent shift in the baseline that persists after the pit
A single unipolar excursion with a slow return
A two-lobed, figure-eight trace as first one coil and then the other passes the pit
No response, because differential coils cancel all flaws
checkQuestions.explanation: Each coil of the opposed pair passes the pit in turn — the first unbalances the bridge one way, the second reverses it — producing the bipolar, figure-eight trace. A persistent baseline shift is absolute-channel behavior at a property change, not a differential pit response.

checkQuestions.question: Gradual, uniform wall thinning along a long length of tube is most reliably tracked by:
options: The absolute channel
The differential self-comparison channel
Either channel equally well
Neither — eddy currents cannot respond to wall loss
checkQuestions.explanation: Both coils of a self-comparison differential pair ride the same slowly changing wall and cancel it; only the absolute channel tracks gradual loss. That complementarity is why in-service examinations record both channels together.

checkQuestions.question: The main reason low-frequency aerospace probes (sliding and ring probes) use a reflection (driver–pickup) winding arrangement is:
options: It increases the standard depth of penetration at a given frequency
It eliminates the need for standardization on a reference part
It makes the probe immune to lift-off variations
Driver and pickup are optimized separately, preserving sensitivity and drift stability over a wide frequency range
checkQuestions.explanation: Depth of penetration is set by frequency, conductivity, and permeability — no winding scheme changes it. Reflection construction lets the driver and pickup each be optimized, so the probe stays sensitive and thermally stable at the very low frequencies buried-layer work requires.

checkQuestions.question: Compared with an induction coil, a Hall element used as the sensing element:
options: Responds to the rate of change of flux, so its output rises with frequency
Responds to flux density directly, so its response holds flat down to DC
Works only on ferromagnetic test materials
Requires no electrical connections to operate
checkQuestions.explanation: The Hall voltage is proportional to flux density itself (Vₕ ∝ I × B), so the response holds from DC up through test frequencies. The coil is the element whose output tracks rate of change and therefore falls as frequency falls — the reason field sensors take over in very-low-frequency, deep-penetration work.

checkQuestions.question: Why can no change of mode, gain, or frequency make a standard bobbin coil reliably detect a purely circumferential crack?
options: The crack lies parallel to the bobbin's circumferential currents, so the coil geometry — not the settings — limits the response
The crack is too deep for any eddy current technique
Tube support plates block the magnetic field completely
Bobbin coils function only in ferromagnetic tubes
checkQuestions.explanation: Signal strength scales with how strongly a flaw interrupts the induced currents. A circumferential crack runs parallel to a bobbin's circumferential currents, so the geometry caps the response no matter the settings. The remedy is a coil whose currents cross the crack: rotating pancake, array, or orthogonal (plus-point) windings.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, ECT Level II Eddy Current Evaluation Course §1.2 (types of eddy current sensing probes)
ASNT SNT-TC-1A (2024), Recommended Practice — Level II interprets and evaluates results and must know the scope and limitations of the method
ASTM E690, Standard Practice for In Situ Electromagnetic (Eddy Current) Examination of Nonmagnetic Heat Exchanger Tubes (bobbin-coil practice)
ASTM E309, Standard Practice for Eddy-Current Examination of Steel Tubular Products Using Magnetic Saturation (encircling-coil practice)
